title: Entropy of Liquids and Glasses from Recurring Structural Patterns
authors: Nina Javerzat, Gerhard Jung, Jorge Kurchan, Misaki Ozawa
abstract: We compute the low-temperature configurational entropy of a two-dimensional supercooled liquid. Our method, based on a higher-dimensional version of the Grassberger--Procaccia algorithm, can be implemented in a manner that is entirely agnostic with respect to both the dynamics and the theoretical framework, as any genuine notion of order should be. In this construction, entropy is obtained as the decay rate of recurrent structural patterns with increasing patch size, directly linking entropy reduction to the growing persistence of amorphous order. Because the method requires only particle positions, without any knowledge of the interaction potential or even of the particle sizes, it can be applied directly to both equilibrium and nonequilibrium aging configurations. The resulting configurational entropy, together with the higher-order Rényi complexities, agree quantitatively with values obtained from conventional definitions. Remarkably, the entropies measured during aging coincide with their equilibrium counterparts when compared at the same inherent-structure energy.
summary: This work develops a novel, structure-only method to calculate the configurational entropy of supercooled liquids and glasses by analyzing the recurrence of structural patterns (patches). By adapting the Grassberger–Procaccia algorithm, the authors show that entropy reduction quantifies the growth of amorphous order, providing a powerful, general diagnostic tool for glassy dynamics.
main_problem: The paper aims to compute the low-temperature configurational entropy of supercooled liquids and glasses and establish a direct link between entropy reduction and the growth of amorphous structural order.
main_result: The method successfully estimates configurational entropy, showing quantitative agreement with conventional definitions, and demonstrates that entropy decreases during aging, coinciding with equilibrium values at the same inherent-structure energy.
method: The core method adapts the Grassberger–Procaccia algorithm to analyze the decay rate of recurrent structural patterns (patches) in particle configurations, yielding both configurational entropy and higher-order Rényi complexities.
model_or_system: The study focuses on two-dimensional supercooled liquids, modeled using a modified ternary Kob–Andersen mixture, and analyzes configurations ranging from equilibrium snapshots to inherent-structure minima.
key_observables: Configurational entropy ($s_{	ext{conf}}$), Rényi complexity ($s_R^{	ext{R\'enyi}, m}$), and the correlation integral $C(d)^m(\epsilon)$.
important_parameters: Low temperatures (approaching $T_g$), patch size ($d$), and the Rényi index ($m$).
main_assumptions: The method assumes that the structural information required for entropy calculation is contained solely within the particle positions, making it agnostic to the interaction potential or dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the correlation integral $C(d)^m(\epsilon)$ for different indices $m$ and structural states (inherent vs. thermal), and show the application of fitting techniques (intercept-fit) to extract complexity measures.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theoretical framework by generalizing the Grassberger-Procaccia algorithm to structural patterns, applies it to calculate both configurational entropy and Rényi complexities, and validates the results by comparing them against established theoretical potentials and analyzing aging dynamics.
why_interesting: While focused on classical structural glass physics, the rigorous application of information-theoretic measures (like Rényi complexity) to characterize the phase space structure of disordered systems is highly relevant to understanding quantum many-body localization and complexity in quantum many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Occurrences of similar patches in an N = 704 configuration. The most similar occurrences, with distance ∆≲0.3, are highlighted for patch sizes d = 14, 18, and 22 in blue, green, and red, respectively. Overlapping patches are omitted for readability.

FIG. 2: Correlation integral C(d) m (ϵ) as a function of the distance threshold ϵ in the vicinity of the scaling regime, for m = 2 at T = 0.047 and several patch sizes d. The straight lines indicate linear fits in the log–log representation, whose slope gives the exponent ν. The inset shows C(d) m (ϵ) over a broader range of ϵ.

FIG. 3: Configurational entropy sconf and R´enyi complexity sR´enyi m=2 , estimated using the Grassberger–Procaccia (GP) algorithm (open circles). The corresponding estimates obtained from the free-energy differences of the quenched and annealed Franz–Parisi (FP) potentials are shown as pentagons, those obtained by direct enumeration of inherent structures (IS) are shown as triangles.

Figure 3 shows the numerically extracted sconf, compared with two independent estimates of the configurational entropy: one obtained from the quenched Franz–Parisi potential, and the other from a direct evaluation of Eq. (1) in which metastable states are approximately identified with inherent structures. We find quantitative agreement among all three estimates. The figure also confirms the theoretical bound sconf ≥sR´enyi m=2 .

FIG. 4: R´enyi complexity sR´enyi 2 measured using the Grassberger–Procaccia (GP) method (open circles) and from inherent-structure (IS) statistics (triangles), plotted as a function of the inherent-structure energy Einh along out-of-equilibrium aging paths at target temperatures T = 0.047 and T = 0.061. The black line shows the complexity for the equilibrium data, at temperatures T < 0.085.
